artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down for a minute before I start the dishwasher. Thanks again for coming over yesterday so I could get through a few work tickets without Jacob hovering. Having you there when he had that little focal spell in the kitchen made it so much less scary.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how we got here. Do you remember that awful night last October, when he had that long seizure in the living room? The one that went over 7 minutes before the Valtoco finally kicked in and we ended up at Edward’s ER? I still have the ER report saved on my laptop in a folder called “Jacob – scary ER visit 10-2023.” There’s a line in there about “possible hypoxic episode – discuss SUDEP risk with family.” I can’t bring myself to read it all the way through, but just knowing it’s there makes my stomach drop.

That’s part of why I’m pushing so hard about Epidiolex at Lurie. At the March 2024 visit with Dr. Hernandez (remember, exam room 7, you came and sat in the corner and took notes), she said that if things don’t improve by 8th grade we may have to look at special ed placement and even evaluate for possible intellectual disability. It’s also in his after-visit summary as “consider neuropsych testing – concern for cognitive impact of ongoing seizures.” I felt like I couldn’t breathe for a second when she said it.

On top of that, work has been tense. Back in November, my manager Elena sent an email titled “Concerns about availability during core hours” and basically warned that if I keep dropping client calls when the school calls about Jacob, it might affect my promotion eligibility this year. I get where she’s coming from, but it feels like I’m being punished for being his mom.

I know you keep saying we’ll figure it out, but sometimes it feels like everything is hanging by a thread—his brain, my job, even my marriage. Michael is so tired all the time. We’re all just stretched.

Anyway, enough heavy stuff. I’ll send you the kids’ spring concert dates once I get them from Lincoln and Elmwood. Maybe we can all go out for ice cream at Oberweis afterward.

Love you,
Laura

Personal note – questions/feelings before next Lurie visit

Appointment: Lurie Children’s – Dr. Hernandez
Date: May 23, 2024
Location: Outpatient neurology, exam room usually 6 or 7.

Things I need to remember to bring up:

1) Epidiolex specifics
- Ask again about liver function tests schedule: baseline, then how often in first 6 months/first year?
- How worried should I be about appetite/weight loss? Jacob already barely eats breakfast.
- Interactions with his current meds: Keppra 750 mg BID, Onfi 10 mg AM/20 mg PM, Lamictal 100 mg BID. Especially Clobazam + Epidiolex = extra sedation?

2) School/cognition
- Remind her of what she said in March 2024 in exam room 7 about possible special ed placement and “consider neuropsych testing – concern for cognitive impact of ongoing seizures.” I’m still replaying that.
- Mention October 2023 504 meeting at Lincoln JH with Ms. Reynolds: MAP scores dropped from 65th → 32nd percentile in math and 58th → 40th in reading. They documented “health-related learning impairment due to epilepsy” in his file. I want her perspective: is this mostly seizures, meds, or both?

3) Safety/long seizures
- The Edward ER visit on 10/14/23 with the “possible hypoxic episode – discuss SUDEP risk with family” note. Ask her directly how worried we should be and what “realistic” risk looks like.

4) My own limits
- Be honest that I’ve been seeing Dr. Rachel Klein at Linden Oaks Behavioral Health every other Thursday at 6 p.m. since March 2024 for anxiety/caregiver burnout (“GAD with panic attacks – caregiver to medically complex child”). It might help her understand why I sound so frantic sometimes.
- Mention that Elena at Blue Oak emailed in November about “Concerns about availability during core hours,” tying Jacob’s seizures to my promotion chances. Better seizure control isn’t just about him; it’s our whole stability.

Remember: breathe. This is a partnership, not an interrogation. Ask for resources for family counseling and local epilepsy support groups in western suburbs.

metadata.title: Prep for May Lurie neurology visit

artifacts.content:
Hi Elena,

I wanted to follow up on your November 3 email (“Concerns about availability during core hours”) and give you some context, as well as propose a more concrete plan going forward.

The three incidents you mentioned where I had to drop client calls were all related to seizure emergencies with my son’s school, Lincoln Junior High here in Naperville. He has difficult-to-control epilepsy and still has focal impaired-awareness seizures 4–6 times a week and generalized tonic-clonic seizures about 1–2 times a month, even on three medications (Keppra, Onfi, Lamictal) plus rescue Diazepam nasal spray.

In October 2023 he had a prolonged generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home that lasted over 7 minutes before the Valtoco worked. We ended up in the Edward Hospital ER, and the note actually says “possible hypoxic episode – discuss SUDEP risk with family.” Since then, whenever the school nurse calls, I have to treat it as potentially serious.

I’m working with his neurologist at Lurie Children’s on adding Epidiolex to hopefully get better control. At our March 2024 visit in exam room 7, the neurologist raised concern that if seizures don’t improve by 8th grade, we may need neuropsych testing and to consider special education placement because of possible long-term cognitive impact. That conversation really drove home that we’re not just talking about a few missed days of school; this affects his entire future.

I share this not to make excuses but to explain why those calls feel so urgent. To minimize disruptions, I’ve started:

- Blocking out 2:30–3:15 p.m. CT in my calendar as “limited availability” since that’s when most school calls come.
- Asking my mom to be on standby one afternoon a week so she can be the first responder if something happens during a client meeting.
- Keeping my phone on silent during critical client demos and listing Michael as primary contact with the school for that hour.

I value my role at Blue Oak and still hope to be considered for promotion in Q2 2024. I’m open to any additional suggestions you have for balancing reliability with my non-negotiable responsibilities as a parent.

Best,
Laura

Hi Sarah,

I owe you about 47 texts, but my thumbs are too tired, so you get an email instead.

Short version: we’re hanging in there. Long version: I am exhausted in my bones.

Jacob is still having 4–6 focal impaired-awareness seizures a week and 1–2 generalized tonic-clonics a month, even with the Keppra/Onfi/Lamictal cocktail and Valtoco as backup. After that awful October 2023 night when he seized for over 7 minutes before the spray worked and the Edward ER note said “possible hypoxic episode – discuss SUDEP risk with family,” something in me broke. I still sleep half-sitting up listening for him.

At school, things are slipping. At the 504/IEP meeting last October in the conference room at Lincoln, the school psychologist, Ms. Reynolds, showed us his MAP scores—math down from the 65th to the 32nd percentile, reading from 58th to 40th. They put “health-related learning impairment due to epilepsy” in his file. Then at the March 2024 Lurie visit in exam room 7, Dr. Hernandez said if seizure control doesn’t improve by 8th grade, we need to think about neuropsych testing and possible intellectual disability/special ed placement. I nodded politely and then cried the whole drive back on I-88.

Meanwhile, work is not exactly a refuge. My manager, Elena, sent that November 3 email about “Concerns about availability during core hours” and basically hinted that if I keep dropping calls when Lincoln’s nurse rings, my Q2 2024 promotion is at risk. So that’s fun.

I finally admitted I can’t white-knuckle this anymore and started seeing a therapist, Dr. Rachel Klein, at Linden Oaks Behavioral Health. Every other Thursday at 6 p.m., I sit in her office and word-vomit about panic attacks every time the school’s number pops up and how guilty I feel about barely having bandwidth for Emily.

Oh, and because we needed one more stressor, Michael and I had a blow-up at my mom’s in Aurora back in February. He actually said, “I can’t keep doing this—sometimes I think about moving closer to my dad in Joliet and starting over.” That night I googled “Illinois legal separation with medically complex child” and even bookmarked some Cook County legal aid page before deleting my history like a teenager.

And yet, life keeps going—Emily has piano, Jacob is obsessed with some new science YouTuber, Daisy still needs walks. If you’re free sometime, I’d love a coffee at Sparrow in downtown Naperville, no kids, just adult conversation.

Love,
Laura

Late-night reflection – don’t send to anyone

Kids are finally asleep, dishwasher humming, Daisy snoring under the table. I’m back at my usual spot with the laptop and a mug of lukewarm tea, trying to make sense of the last year.

I keep opening that Edward Hospital PDF from the October 2023 ER visit—“Jacob – scary ER visit 10-2023” buried in my Documents folder—and then slamming it shut when I see the phrase “possible hypoxic episode – discuss SUDEP risk with family.” It feels like holding a grenade. Part of me thinks if I don’t read it, maybe it’s not real.

Then there’s the March 2024 Lurie appointment, exam room 7. Dr. Hernandez, kind but blunt, talking about “consider neuropsych testing – concern for cognitive impact of ongoing seizures” and maybe needing special ed placement and evaluation for intellectual disability by 8th grade if things don’t improve. I remember staring at the cartoon fish on the wall and thinking, “This is my 12-year-old we’re talking about, not a case study.”

Work is its own slow drip of dread. Elena’s November 3 email—“Concerns about availability during core hours”—is still starred in my Blue Oak inbox. One more seizure call from Lincoln in the middle of a client demo and my Q2 2024 promotion is gone. How do you choose between your kid’s airway and your career ladder?

I finally admitted the panic attacks aren’t just “being tired” and started seeing Dr. Rachel Klein at Linden Oaks Behavioral Health every other Thursday at 6 p.m. She wrote “GAD with panic attacks – caregiver to medically complex child” on her intake form, and seeing it in black and white weirdly helped. There’s a name for the way my heart races when the school’s number pops up.

The worst night this year was that argument at Mom’s in Aurora in February. Michael at her kitchen table saying, “I can’t keep doing this—sometimes I think about moving closer to my dad in Joliet and starting over.” Later, after everyone went to bed, I sat right here and googled “Illinois legal separation with medically complex child” and bookmarked some Cook County legal aid page. I haven’t looked at it since, but the fact that I did it at all scares me.

Some nights I even draft emails to Pastor Mark at Grace Community with subject lines like “Struggling with faith after Jacob’s seizures.” I type things like, “I’m ashamed to admit it, but sometimes I resent God for letting this happen to my son,” and then I close the window without saving.

Tomorrow morning, I’ll pack Jacob’s Keppra, Onfi, Lamictal, and the bright red Valtoco pouch, make lunches, answer tickets for Blue Oak, and smile at Emily over cereal. No one at drop-off will know any of this is rattling around in my head.

Event: Therapy with Dr. Klein – caregiver burnout/anxiety

Date: Thursday, June 13, 2024
Time: 6:00–6:50 p.m.
Location: Linden Oaks Behavioral Health, Naperville – outpatient offices
Attendees: Laura Anderson, Dr. Rachel Klein

Notes:
- Standing appointment every other Thursday at 6 p.m. since March 2024.
- Diagnosis Dr. Klein uses in her notes: “GAD with panic attacks – caregiver to medically complex child.” It still feels strange seeing that phrase, but also validating.

Agenda for this session:
1) Process last week’s near-miss at work
   - Got a call from Lincoln Junior High nurse right in the middle of a Blue Oak client check-in. Heart in throat remembering Elena’s November 3 email (“Concerns about availability during core hours,” warning that continued disruptions could affect Q2 2024 promotion).
   - Talk about strategies so I don’t instantly go into fight-or-flight every time the school’s number shows up.

2) Medical anxiety
   - Revisit lingering fear from the October 2023 Edward ER visit (prolonged generalized tonic-clonic seizure >7 minutes before Valtoco worked; note saying “possible hypoxic episode – discuss SUDEP risk with family”).
   - How to live with that knowledge without checking Jacob’s breathing 20 times a night.

3) Future worries
   - March 2024 Lurie neurology appointment in exam room 7: Dr. Hernandez mentioning “consider neuropsych testing – concern for cognitive impact of ongoing seizures” and potential need for special education placement/intellectual disability evaluation by 8th grade.
   - October 2023 504/IEP meeting at Lincoln: Ms. Reynolds showing MAP score drops (math 65th → 32nd percentile, reading 58th → 40th) and adding “health-related learning impairment due to epilepsy” to his file.

4) Marriage/family stress
   - February 2024 argument at Mom’s in Aurora where Michael said, “I can’t keep doing this—sometimes I think about moving closer to my dad in Joliet and starting over.”
   - The night I googled “Illinois legal separation with medically complex child” and bookmarked a Cook County legal aid page.

Remember to leave the house by 5:30 p.m. so I can swing by Mom’s in Aurora to drop the kids and the red Valtoco pouch, plus Jacob’s evening Keppra/Onfi/Lamictal.

Hi Emily (social work dept.),

Thank you again for talking with me briefly after Jacob’s EEG last month. You mentioned I could reach out if we had questions about costs for new medications.

Jacob’s neurologist, Dr. Hernandez at Lurie Children’s, is recommending we start Epidiolex soon because his seizures are still not well controlled on Keppra, Onfi, and Lamictal. I’m nervous about adding another expensive drug when we’re already stretched.

In January 2024 we got a $1,947 bill from Lurie for Jacob’s EEG and MRI. I ended up calling Blue Cross Blue Shield of Illinois from my home office and setting up a 12‑month interest-free payment plan because we just didn’t have enough in savings to pay it outright. The rep on the phone mentioned our account is now flagged for “high utilization of pediatric neurology services,” which made me worry about how future approvals will go.

I’m trying to be proactive before we get blindsided by another big bill. A few questions:

1) Do you know if BCBS of IL typically requires prior authorization for Epidiolex? If so, is that something your office initiates or does Dr. Hernandez’s team handle it fully?

2) Are there manufacturer co-pay assistance programs or hospital-based financial assistance options for families like us who are insured but still struggling with out-of-pocket costs? I saw some mention of programs on the Epidiolex website but it’s hard to tell what’s legitimate.

3) Given that we’re already on the 12‑month plan for the $1,947 EEG/MRI bill, is there any way to bundle future neurology-related charges or at least avoid going into collections if we need another payment plan?

4) Lastly, are there any support groups (in person around Naperville/Aurora or virtual) for parents of kids with difficult-to-control epilepsy? Beyond the financial stress, this has been emotionally draining for our whole family. I’ve started seeing a therapist myself at Linden Oaks in Naperville, which helps, but I think connecting with other parents would be good too.

I really appreciate any guidance or resources you can share. We want to do everything we can for Jacob while still keeping our heads above water financially.

Best,
Laura Anderson
Naperville, IL

metadata.subject: Questions re: Epidiolex costs and assistance options
